name: Two Methods to Cure Olives
recipeInstructions:
To salt cure ripe olives:
1. Use small, unbruised black or purplish-black olives.
2. Cover the bottom of a clean slatted wooden box (such as a fruit lug or flat rectangular basket lined with double-layer cheesecloth).
3. Rinse the olives in cold water and layer them at the bottom of the box.
4. Cover the damp olives with a layer of uniodized or pickling salt. Use at least one pound of salt for each pound of olives for the month-long process.
5. Repeat layering of olives and salt, then cover the entire batch with another layer of salt and cloth.
6. Store the box in a tray or cardboard box to prevent staining the countertop or floor. Keep it in a cool basement or garage.
7. After one week, uncover the olives and transfer them along with the salt to another container.
8. Re-layer the olives with additional salt in the same cloth-lined box for about three to four days.
9. Repeat layering and salting every four days for one month, using new salt each time until the olives are wrinkled and lose bitterness.
10. If mold appears on the olives, rinse it off with cold water and soak them in a bowl of distilled white vinegar for one hour before returning to the salt cure.
11. Remove cured olives from salt; place them in a large strainer or colander.
12. Dip the strainer or colander into boiling water for a few seconds, then drain.
13. Rinse the blanched olives with cold tap water. Repeat this step with remaining olives, changing boiling water whenever it becomes too salty after about every 12th dip.
14. Spread the olives out on paper towels to dry for a few hours or overnight.
15. Place the dried olives in jars and cover with olive oil. Mix in herbs, hot peppers, garlic, vinegar, or grated orange or lemon rind if desired.
16. Store sealed jars in the refrigerator. The olives will keep for at least one month. Dry-cured olives may also be stored in new salt in airtight containers for up to six months in the refrigerator.
To brine cure green or ripe olives:
1. Use mature, fully colored green or dark-red to purplish-black olives.
2. The ripe olives may fade in color during curing but will darken again when exposed to air.
3. Slash each olive to its pit three times with a small knife.
4. Rinse the olives in water and place them in one-quart glass jars, filling them 3/4 full.
5. Cover the olives with a brine containing dissolved uniodized or pickling salt (3/4 cup per gallon of water).
6. Insert a small, sealed food-storage bag filled with 1/4 cup water into each jar to keep the olives immersed in brine.
7. Screw on the lid loosely and refrigerate for one week.
8. Replace the brine with a mixture of 1 2/3 cups salt per gallon of water after 15 days, and continue replacing it at one-month intervals for additional two to three months.
9. Store olives in brine while keeping them airtight. They can be stored for at least one year.
10. Olives can be eaten within two months if you prefer fairly-bitter olives. Use them for cooking or as appetizers. To reduce saltiness, soak the olives in fresh water for three days before storing any uneaten desalted olives in a solution of one part red or white wine vinegar to two parts water, floating a layer of olive oil on top to preserve them.
